edges.description: HBV infection triggers persistent immune activation and hepatic inflammation through both innate and adaptive immune responses.
A fraction of viral DNA integrates into host genome at sites of DNA damage, contributing to persistent HBsAg expression and oncogenesis.
DNA integration drives oncogenesis through activation of TERT and other proto-oncogenes, genomic instability, and structural rearrangements.
Chronic inflammation activates hepatic stellate cells leading to extracellular matrix deposition and progressive fibrosis.
Progressive fibrosis leads to architectural disruption and nodule formation characteristic of cirrhosis.
Progressive fibrosis leads to complete architectural disruption and end-stage liver disease.

nodes.description: Persistent immune response against HBV-infected hepatocytes leads to liver damage mediated by T cells, NK cells, and Kupffer cells.

evidence.snippet: The immune response to HBV-encoded antigens is responsible both for viral clearance and for disease pathogenesis during this infection.
Cirrhosis is widely prevalent worldwide and can be a consequence of different causes, such as... hepatitis B or C infection.

evidence.reference_title: Persistent Low Level of Hepatitis B Virus Promotes Fibrosis Progression During Therapy.
evidence.snippet: Progression of liver fibrosis still occurs in some patients with chronic hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection despite antiviral therapy.

evidence.reference_title: Insights into the impact of hepatitis B virus on hepatic stellate cell activation.
evidence.snippet: During chronic hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection, hepatic fibrosis is a serious pathological condition caused by virus-induced liver damage.

evidence.reference_title: Immune suppression in chronic hepatitis B infection associated liver disease: A review.
evidence.snippet: Hepatitis B virus (HBV) infection is one the leading risk factors for chronic hepatitis, liver fibrosis, cirrhosis and hepatocellular cancer (HCC).

nodes.description: Extensive fibrosis and scarring significantly impair liver function.

evidence.reference_title: Hepatitis B and end-stage liver disease.
evidence.snippet: Untreated, chronic hepatitis B acquired early in life results in cirrhosis, liver failure, or hepatocellular carcinoma in up to 40% of individuals.
Cirrhosis is defined histologically as an advanced form of progressive hepatic fibrosis with distortion of the hepatic architecture and regenerative nodule formation.

evidence.reference_title: Liver fibrosis and chronic viral hepatitis.
evidence.snippet: Liver fibrosis results from chronic damage to the liver in conjunction with the progressive accumulation of fibrillar extracellular matrix proteins.

evidence.reference_title: Regression of fibrosis after HBV antiviral therapy. Is cirrhosis reversible?
evidence.snippet: Long-lasting HBV-DNA suppression is considered to be the best surrogate end-point of antiviral therapy in patients with hepatitis B virus (HBV) related chronic hepatitis or cirrhosis, and it is a prerequisite to prevent liver-related complications and improve survival.

nodes.description: Integration of viral dslDNA into host genome occurs at sites of DNA damage, driving insertional mutagenesis, structural rearrangements, and activation of proto-oncogenes.

HBV enters hepatocytes via NTCP receptor binding, and viral rcDNA is converted to covalently closed circular DNA (cccDNA) which forms a stable chromatinized minichromosome in the nucleus, enabling persistent infection.

evidence.reference_title: Hepatitis B Virus DNA Integration and Clonal Expansion of Hepatocytes in the Chronically Infected Liver.
evidence.snippet: Human hepatitis B virus (HBV) can cause chronic, lifelong infection of the liver that may lead to persistent or episodic immune-mediated inflammation against virus-infected hepatocytes.

evidence.reference_title: Hepatitis B virus biology and life cycle.
evidence.snippet: 'Hepatitis B virus (HBV) specifically infects hepatocytes and causes severe liver diseases.

evidence.reference_title: Spatiotemporal Differences in Presentation of CD8 T Cell Epitopes during Hepatitis B Virus Infection.
evidence.snippet: Distinct populations of hepatocytes infected with hepatitis B virus (HBV) or only harboring HBV DNA integrations coexist within an HBV chronically infected liver.

evidence.reference_title: The Interactions between HBV and the Innate Immunity of Hepatocytes.
evidence.snippet: HBV is a major cause of cirrhosis and hepatocellular carcinoma.

nodes.description: Chronic inflammation promotes fibrotic tissue deposition replacing healthy liver cells.

evidence.reference_title: Liver fibrosis and chronic viral hepatitis.
evidence.snippet: Liver fibrosis results from chronic damage to the liver in conjunction with the progressive accumulation of fibrillar extracellular matrix proteins.

evidence.reference_title: Chronic inflammation involves CCL11 and IL-13 to facilitate the development of liver cirrhosis and fibrosis in chronic hepatitis B virus infection.
evidence.snippet: Chronic inflammation involves CCL11 and IL-13 to facilitate the development of liver cirrhosis and fibrosis in chronic hepatitis B virus infection.

evidence.reference_title: [Inflammation and Hepatic Fibrosis, Then Hepatocellular Carcinoma].
evidence.snippet: Chronic unresolved inflammation is associated with persistent hepatic injury and concurrent regeneration, leading to sequential development of fibrosis, cirrhosis, and eventually HCC.

evidence.reference_title: Platelet activation during chronic hepatitis B infection exacerbates liver inflammation and promotes fibrosis.
evidence.snippet: Recurrent hepatitis activity during chronic hepatitis B virus infection results in fibrosis and even hepatocellular carcinoma.
